title: 20260127_Press_release_3_BCA
text:
City Atrium T +32 2 277 52 72 info@bma-abc.be 
Rue du Progrès 50 http://www.belgiancompetition.be 
1210 Brussels 

PRESS RELEASE 
N° 3/2026 
27 January 2026 

The Belgian Competition Authority approves Delhaize’s acquisition of Delfood and the Louis Delhaize 
retail chain, under conditions 
 
On 26 January 2026, the Belgian Competition Authority ("BCA") approved the acquisition by Delhaize of Delfood, 
owner of the Louis Delhaize retail chain subject to, among other conditions, the divestment of a number of 
proximity stores and petrol station shops in certain local markets. 
Delhaize is a Belgian company active in the distribution of food and other daily consumer goods in retail stores 
operated mainly under franchise. Delhaize's 745 stores are operated under various brands, namely AD Delhaize, 
Proxy Delhaize and Shop&Go Delhaize. 
Delfood is a Belgian company active in the distribution of food and other daily consumer goods, both at 
wholesale and retail level, mainly through the Louis Delhaize chain. The Louis Delhaize network consists of 322 
stores. Some of these stores are operated by franchisees. Other shops have a supply agreement or operate 
under a framework agreement concluded through a petrol station network. 
The transaction in question pertains to the acquisition of the entirety of Delfood’s activities by Delhaize, 
including the Louis Delhaize retail network but also its wholesale activities. Given the respective and combined 
turnover of Delfood and Delhaize, the transaction was subject to prior approval by the BCA under merger 
control. 
Given the respective activities of Delhaize and Delfood, the acquisition was scrutinized from the point of view of 
its possible impact on (i) the Belgian market for the wholesale distribution of food and non-food products, (ii) 
the local markets for the retail distribution of everyday consumer goods in proximity stores, and (iii) the local 
markets for the retail distribution of everyday consumer goods in petrol station shops outside motorways. The 
notion of 'local market' refers in this context to a store's catchment area, i.e., the geographical area within which 
a store attracts the majority of its customers and where the store generates most of its revenues. 
During the merger control review process, particular attention was paid to the possible impact of the transaction 
on prices and the quality of supply at retail level, as well as to the impact on stores supplied by Delhaize or 
Delfood while not under their control. 
Delhaize and Delfood are direct competitors in the market for the sale of everyday consumer goods in proximity 
stores and petrol station shops. The loss of competition between the two retail chains eliminates significant 
competitive pressure in some local markets. The loss of competitive pressure is not sufficiently offset by other 
competing stores. In addition, the investigation of the transaction also revealed concerns about the situation of 
stores supplied by Delhaize or Delfood. 

City Atrium T +32 2 277 52 72 info@bma-abc.be 
Rue du Progrès 50 http://www.belgiancompetition.be 
1210 Brussels 
Due to these potential negative competitive effects , the BCA concluded that there were serious doubts about 
the admissibility of the transaction. These serious doubts were communicated to Delhaize, which subsequently 
offered commitments to address the BCA's concerns. 
The commitments include, in particular, the divestment to another franchisor of a number of proximity stores 
and petrol station shops in certain local markets. The commitments further provide that other stores, which are 
supplied or commercially supervised by Delhaize or Delfood, will be granted more contractual freedom vis-à-vis 
Delhaize. This will allow them to compete freely with shops belonging to the Delhaize retail networks. Stores 
supplied by Delhaize or Delfood without belonging to their franchise networks will also be guaranteed fair, 
reasonable and non-discriminatory supply conditions, in line with existing supply conditions. Finally, if Delhaize 
wishes to integrate certain stores under supply contract within its own franchise networks, it will first h ave to 
seek the authorization of the BCA . This will enable the BCA to verify whether the inclusion of the store(s) in 
question in the Delhaize franchise networks could lead to excessive concentration in certain local supply areas. 
Following a thorough analysis, the commitments were deemed sufficient to remove the BCA’s concerns, which 
enabled the approval of the transaction. 
 
For more information, please contact: 
Axel Desmedt - President 
Tel: + 32 (2) 277 92 80 
E-mail: axel.desmedt@bma-abc.be 

The Belgian Competition Authority (BCA) is an independent administrative authority that contributes to the definition 
and implementation of competition policy in Belgium. Concretely, the BCA pursues anti -competitive practices, such 
as cartels and abuses of a dominant position, and reviews the main merger operations. The BCA cooperates with the 
other competition authorities of the member states of the European Union and the European Commission within the 
European Competition Network (ECN).
